text:
HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY SURGERY — POSTOPERATIVE CLINIC NOTE
Two-Week Postoperative Follow-Up: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple Procedure)

---

ATTENDING SURGEON: [Hepatopancreatobiliary Surgery]
SERVICE: Hepatopancreatobiliary / Surgical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Postoperative outpatient follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski, a 67-year-old male, presents to the hepatopancreatobiliary surgery clinic for his two-week postoperative follow-up after undergoing an open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental superior mesenteric vein (SMV) resection and primary reconstruction for pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. He reports fatigue, mild epigastric discomfort, and decreased appetite. He was discharged from the hospital approximately two days ago with a closed-suction abdominal drain in situ.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski has a well-documented history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head, initially diagnosed approximately seven months ago at age 66. At diagnosis, his CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,200 U/mL, and cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees and short-segment abutment of the SMV-portal vein confluence, consistent with borderline resectable disease by NCCN/AHPBA criteria. Two periportal lymph nodes were radiographically suspicious. Molecular profiling of the EUS-FNA specimen revealed KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; the tumor was microsatellite stable, HRD-negative, and TMB-low with no actionable targeted therapy identified.

He was initially treated with biliary decompression via ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement given obstructive jaundice (total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL). Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, he underwent neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) for 8 cycles over approximately 4 months, with an oxaliplatin dose reduction to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 4 due to cumulative grade 2–3 peripheral sensory neuropathy, and irinotecan reduction to 135 mg/m2 (75% dose) after cycle 7 due to a grade 3 diarrhea episode requiring hospitalization. CA 19-9 declined dramatically from 1,200 U/mL at baseline to 85 U/mL after cycle 8, and restaging pancreatic protocol CT showed tumor reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with improved vascular abutment.

Following the tumor board reassessment confirming excellent biochemical and radiographic response, he proceeded to consolidative long-course chemoradiation: IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine (reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID due to grade 1–2 hand-foot syndrome). Pre-surgical restaging CT after chemoradiation showed further tumor reduction to 2.1 cm, essentially restored SMA fat plane with estimated residual abutment under 60 degrees, and patent SMV-PV confluence. CA 19-9 at that point was 62 U/mL.

He was brought to the operating room and underwent open pancreaticoduodenectomy. Intraoperatively, the SMA was successfully dissected free via complete medial dissection without requiring arterial reconstruction. The SMV demonstrated a short segment of desmoplastic adhesion, necessitating segmental SMV resection with primary end-to-end reconstruction, performed with vascular surgery assistance. No peritoneal metastases or gross hepatic metastases were identified. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin were negative. Reconstruction was performed in standard fashion: duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss was 650 mL; two units of packed red blood cells were transfused intraoperatively. Total operative time was 6 hours 45 minutes.

Final surgical pathology confirmed: 2.0 cm residual moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive fibrosis and treatment effect (CAP tumor response grade 2, moderate response). All surgical margins were negative (R0 resection): pancreatic neck margin > 1 mm, posterior margin 1.5 mm, anterior margin 2.1 mm, SMV groove margin 1.0 mm. Lymph node status: 3/18 nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN1). Perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion both present. Pathologic staging: ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition, post-treatment).

Postoperative hospital course: He was in the surgical ICU for the first two days, then transferred to the surgical floor. His diet was advanced from clear liquids to a soft diet by postoperative day 5 without significant difficulty. He developed delayed gastric emptying (grade A per ISGPS criteria), managed conservatively with nasogastric decompression and metoclopramide, which resolved by approximately postoperative day 10. Drain amylase checked on postoperative day 3 was elevated at 1,850 U/L (greater than three times the upper limit of normal), raising concern for early pancreatic fistula formation, and the drain was accordingly left in situ. He was discharged on postoperative day 12 with the drain in place and is returning today for wound inspection, drain assessment, and discussion of next steps including adjuvant therapy planning.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Significant fatigue, reported at 5–6 out of 10 in severity. No fevers at home (denies fever > 38.0°C by home thermometer). Night sweats absent.
- GI: Decreased appetite; consuming approximately 50–60% of normal intake. Tolerating soft, low-fat diet. Reports loose stools, 2–3 per day, non-bloody; consistent with exocrine insufficiency and/or postoperative bowel changes. Mild epigastric discomfort, rated 3/10, non-radiating. No nausea currently. No vomiting. Denies obstipation or inability to pass flatus.
- Drain: Drain site mildly tender. Output described by patient as approximately 50–75 mL/day of yellowish-pink fluid since discharge. No frankly purulent output noted by patient.
- Wound: Incision site mildly uncomfortable; no erythema or drainage noted by patient.
- Neurologic: Bilateral distal lower extremity numbness and tingling, consistent with pre-existing oxaliplatin-related peripheral neuropathy; stable, no worsening.
- Endocrine/metabolic: Checking blood glucose with home glucometer; values ranging from 130–250 mg/dL on his current regimen of basal insulin (glargine). Mealtime aspart not yet optimally dosed.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
- Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea with ambulation — attributed to deconditioning. No cough.
- Genitourinary: Normal urinary output, no dysuria.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A-mutated), ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB post-treatment — as detailed above
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus — recently transitioned to insulin-dependent (post-pancreatectomy diabetes/worsened endogenous insulin capacity); previously managed with metformin
- Hypertension — managed with antihypertensive therapy (held perioperatively, to be restarted)
- Obstructive jaundice — resolved following biliary stenting; stent removed prior to surgery
- Delayed gastric emptying (grade A) — resolved postoperatively
- Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy — grade 2 at peak, currently grade 1–2
- No prior abdominal surgeries prior to the Whipple procedure

SMOKING/SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired pipe fitter
- 30 pack-year smoking history; quit approximately 15 years prior
- Lives with spouse; adequate home support confirmed
- No alcohol or illicit drug use documented

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No family history of pancreatic cancer
- Family history of hypertension (father)

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS (current at the time of visit):
1. Insulin glargine (basal) — dose per endocrinology titration
2. Insulin aspart (mealtime) — sliding scale, not yet fully optimized
3. Creon (pancreatic enzyme replacement, 36,000 lipase units per meal, 18,000 units with snacks)
4. Ondansetron 4 mg orally PRN nausea
5. Metoclopramide 5 mg orally TID (being tapered off; prescribed for delayed gastric emptying)
6. Lisinopril 10 mg daily (antihypertensive — held immediately postoperatively, resumed at discharge)
7. Proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 40 mg daily) — standard post-Whipple
8. Duloxetine 30 mg daily — for neuropathic pain management (started during adjuvant phase per neurology)
9. Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN anxiety/sleep (low-dose)
10. Multivitamin
11. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (vitamins A, D, E, K) — given exocrine insufficiency

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Mr. Kowalski is a thin-appearing older male in no acute distress. He appears fatigued but is alert, oriented x4, and in good spirits. He is somewhat pale.

Vitals:
- Temperature: 37.2°C
- Blood pressure: 118/72 mmHg
- Heart rate: 78 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- SpO2: 98% on room air
- Weight: [recorded; approximately 8 kg below pre-diagnosis baseline; stable since discharge]

Head and Neck: Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated. No jaundice.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or rales. Mild splinting with deep inspiration.

Abdomen:
- Abdomen soft, mildly tender to palpation in the epigastric and right upper quadrant regions — consistent with expected post-surgical discomfort.
- No rebound tenderness or guarding.
- Midline surgical incision is intact, approximately 22 cm in length; staples/sutures in place and intact. No erythema, induration, or dehiscence. No wound drainage noted.
- Jackson-Pratt drain in right upper quadrant position; insertion site without surrounding erythema or purulence. Drain output since drainage bag last emptied (24 hours): approximately 60 mL of serosanguinous/yellowish fluid.
- Bowel sounds present and active in all quadrants.
- No palpable mass, organomegaly, or guarding.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral feet to the ankle level (pre-existing oxaliplatin neuropathy, stable).

Neurologic: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Bilateral distal lower extremity sensory loss as noted.

Skin: Mild pallor. No jaundice. No petechiae. Right upper quadrant drain site clean.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 9.1 x 10^9/L (within normal limits; no leukocytosis)
- Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (mild anemia — consistent with postoperative/perioperative blood loss and nutritional status; improvement from immediate postoperative value)
- Platelets: 312 x 10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (baseline; within normal limits)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normalized; previously elevated pre-stenting to 2.1)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.3 mg/dL
- ALT: 34 U/L
- AST: 29 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 82 U/L
- Albumin: 2.9 g/dL (low — reflects nutritional depletion from prolonged treatment course and perioperative stress)
- Total protein: 5.8 g/dL
- Glucose (fasting): 168 mg/dL (suboptimal glycemic control, as expected; endocrinology will co-manage)

Tumor Marker:
- CA 19-9: 28 U/mL (within normal range; appropriately declining postoperatively)

Drain Fluid Amylase (sent from drain collected this morning):
- Drain amylase: 1,420 U/L (elevated; > 3x upper limit of serum amylase; consistent with ongoing biochemical pancreatic fistula)

Coagulation:
- PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

No new cross-sectional imaging obtained at today's visit. The most recent pre-visit imaging was the pre-surgical restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (performed approximately 6 weeks prior to surgery), which demonstrated a 2.1 cm pancreatic head mass with largely restored SMA fat plane, patent SMV-PV confluence, no hepatic metastases, and no peritoneal implants. Intraoperative findings and final pathology supersede this imaging for the current oncologic assessment. Surveillance imaging will be planned going forward.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 67-year-old male with ypT2N1M0 Stage IIB pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative) of the pancreatic head, now approximately two weeks following open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental SMV resection and primary reconstruction, achieving a complete R0 resection. His postoperative course was notable for delayed gastric emptying (grade A, now resolved) and persistently elevated drain amylase consistent with an early-grade pancreatic fistula. He presents today in a generally stable condition but with several ongoing management issues:

1. PANCREATIC FISTULA / ELEVATED DRAIN AMYLASE:
Today's drain amylase returns at 1,420 U/L, confirming persistent biochemical leak from the pancreaticojejunostomy. The patient remains afebrile and hemodynamically stable today, without leukocytosis, frank sepsis, or clinical signs of intra-abdominal collection. Drain output is approximately 50–75 mL/day per patient report. At this point, the clinical picture is most consistent with an International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula (ISGPS) grade A or early grade B fistula. Clinical vigilance is mandatory given the SMAD4 deletion in his tumor (which historically associates with early systemic spread, though surgical pathology confirmed R0 resection) — we must be careful not to misattribute constitutional symptoms.
- Plan: Drain will be kept in place. Patient and spouse instructed on drain care, monitoring for signs of infection (fever > 38.3°C, drain site erythema, change in drain output character), and when to call or present to ED. CA 19-9 will be followed as marker of disease status, not fistula.
- Patient will be seen again in one week for drain reassessment. Serial drain amylase will be rechecked. If drain amylase fails to trend down or patient develops fever, leukocytosis, or signs of a peri-anastomotic collection, cross-sectional imaging (CT abdomen with IV contrast) will be obtained and infectious disease/interventional radiology consulted as appropriate.
- Low-fat oral diet encouraged to minimize pancreatic stimulation; Creon continued with all meals.
- Octreotide is not being initiated at this time given the patient remains clinically stable; threshold for initiation will be low if fistula output increases or patient becomes symptomatic.

2. NUTRITION AND WEIGHT:
Patient has lost approximately 8 kg from pre-diagnosis baseline, reflecting the combined effects of months of aggressive neoadjuvant therapy, surgery, and exocrine insufficiency. Albumin at 2.9 g/dL confirms nutritional depletion. He is currently on a soft, low-fat diet.
- Plan: Referred to dietitian for individualized post-Whipple dietary counseling, including guidance on small frequent meals, low-fat diet, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation, and expected dietary progression.
- Pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon 36,000 lipase units/meal) to be optimized — dose may need upward adjustment. Patient to keep food diary and report steatorrhea or worsening diarrhea.
- Encourage high-protein, calorie-dense foods as tolerated.
- Consider outpatient oral nutritional supplementation (e.g., Ensure/Boost high protein).

3. GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT / POST-PANCREATECTOMY DIABETES:
Patient has pre-existing type 2 diabetes, now significantly worsened due to loss of pancreatic beta-cell mass following Whipple procedure (effectively a post-pancreatectomy diabetes overlay). Fasting glucose today 168 mg/dL; home readings ranging 130–250 mg/dL.
- Plan: Formal endocrinology co-management to be maintained/re-engaged. Metformin appropriately held (exocrine insufficiency + post-surgical period); ongoing insulin (glargine + aspart) regimen continuation with titration.
- Patient educated on hypoglycemia avoidance given impaired glucagon response post-pancreatectomy.

4. WOUND CARE:
Incision healing well. No signs of superficial surgical site infection.
- Plan: Wound staples/sutures to be removed at this visit. Patient instructed on wound care; may shower, pat dry.

5. ANEMIA:
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL — mild anemia, likely multifactorial (perioperative blood loss, nutritional deficiency, anemia of inflammation). Patient asymptomatic at rest; mild exertional dyspnea consistent with deconditioning and mild anemia.
- Plan: Recheck CBC in 4–6 weeks. Iron studies and B12/folate levels ordered. Oral iron supplementation to be considered if iron-deficient (absorption may be impaired post-Whipple given altered duodenal anatomy).

6. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY:
Grade 1–2 bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, residual from cumulative oxaliplatin exposure, remains stable. Duloxetine 30 mg daily ongoing.
- Plan: Continue current regimen; neurology co-management ongoing.

7. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PLANNING:
Final pathology confirms ypT2N1M0 (Stage IIB) with R0 resection, 3/18 positive lymph nodes, perineural and lymphovascular invasion — high-risk features with significant recurrence risk despite excellent response to neoadjuvant therapy. SMAD4 deletion is associated with propensity for systemic spread and mandates diligent surveillance and completion of adjuvant therapy.
- Plan: Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (per the APACT regimen) is planned. Per NCCN guidelines and the APACT trial data, adjuvant gem/capecitabine is appropriate for this patient. Target start: approximately 6–8 weeks postoperatively, contingent on resolution of the pancreatic fistula and adequate nutritional/performance status recovery.
- Medical oncology has been notified of the operative details and pathologic stage. Medical oncology follow-up appointment scheduled for approximately 4–6 weeks postoperatively.
- Germline BRCA1/2 and ATM testing was recommended at tumor board — if not yet completed, this should be coordinated with medical oncology (results were reported as wild-type on somatic NGS, but germline testing is separately warranted per NCCN guidelines for pancreatic adenocarcinoma).

8. VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM PROPHYLAXIS:
Patient with known cancer post-abdominal-pelvic surgery; continued VTE risk.
- Plan: Continue low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) prophylaxis per postoperative protocol for extended period (up to 4 weeks postoperatively per current guidelines); transition to patient monitoring thereafter.

9. FOLLOW-UP:
- Return to hepatopancreatobiliary surgery clinic in one week for drain reassessment and drain amylase recheck.
- Medical oncology follow-up in 4–6 weeks for adjuvant therapy planning.
- Labs (CBC, CMP, CA 19-9, iron studies, B12/folate) to be rechecked at next visit or prior to medical oncology appointment.
- Endocrinology: existing follow-up to be maintained.
- Neurology: existing follow-up to be maintained.
- Dietitian referral placed today.

Mr. Kowalski was seen for a total of 45 minutes. The case complexity, drain management, and multidisciplinary coordination were discussed at length with patient and his spouse. Questions were answered. He verbalized understanding of the drain care plan and return precautions.

Electronically signed by: [Hepatopancreatobiliary Surgery Attending]
